syrupCalculator.title: Syrup Calculator
syrupCalculator.description: Calculate sugar and water amounts for feeding syrup
syrupCalculator.intro: Free sugar syrup calculator for beekeepers. Use 1:1 (thin) syrup for spring stimulation, 3:2 (medium) as an all-purpose feed, and 2:1 (thick) for fall feeding to build winter stores. Pick a container size and ratio — exact sugar and water weights are computed from sucrose displacement so the finished syrup fills your container.
syrupCalculator.unitsDescription: Switch between metric and imperial measurements
syrupCalculator.ratioDescription: Sugar to water ratio (by weight to volume)
syrupCalculator.containerDescription: Total volume of syrup to prepare
syrupCalculator.step2: Heat the water until warm (not boiling)
syrupCalculator.step4: Stir until the sugar is completely dissolved
syrupCalculator.step5: Let the syrup cool to room temperature before feeding
syrupCalculator.tip1Description: Best for spring feeding to stimulate brood rearing and simulate a nectar flow. Use when bees need encouragement to build up.
syrupCalculator.tip2Description: A good all-purpose syrup. Works well for both spring stimulation and light fall feeding.
syrupCalculator.tip3Description: Best for fall/autumn feeding to build up winter stores. Bees can store it quickly with less effort to evaporate excess water.
syrupCalculator.tip4Description: Always use white granulated sugar — never brown sugar, molasses, or artificial sweeteners. Do not boil the syrup as it can create HMF (hydroxymethylfurfural) which is toxic to bees. Feed in the evening to reduce robbing.

items.question: What sugar-to-water ratio should I use to feed bees?
items.answer: Use 1:1 syrup (equal parts sugar and water by weight) in spring to stimulate brood rearing, 3:2 as a general-purpose feed, and 2:1 (two parts sugar to one part water) in fall to build winter stores. Thick syrup is easier for bees to convert to capped stores; thin syrup mimics a nectar flow.

items.question: When should I feed bees thin (1:1) syrup?
items.answer: Feed 1:1 syrup in spring or whenever you want to stimulate brood rearing. Its water content mimics a natural nectar flow, encouraging the queen to lay and the colony to build up.

items.question: When should I feed bees thick (2:1) syrup?
items.answer: Feed 2:1 syrup in late summer and fall to help colonies build winter stores. The lower water content means bees spend less energy evaporating it before capping.

items.question: Can I use brown sugar, honey, or molasses to make bee syrup?
items.answer: No. Use only white granulated cane or beet sugar. Brown sugar, molasses, and unrefined sweeteners contain compounds that cause dysentery in bees. Honey from unknown sources can also transmit disease.

items.question: Should I boil sugar syrup for bees?
items.answer: No. Heat the water until warm enough to dissolve the sugar, but do not boil. Boiling sugar syrup produces hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF), which is toxic to bees.

items.question: How much sugar do I need for one liter of 1:1 syrup?
items.answer: For one liter of finished 1:1 syrup, you need roughly 715 g of water and 715 g of white granulated sugar. The exact amounts depend on the ratio and final volume — this calculator computes them based on sucrose displacement.

liebefelder.title: Liebefelder Schätzmethode
liebefelder.intro: The Liebefelder method estimates colony strength by assessing comb area in eighths (1/8 steps): how much is covered with bees, brood, pollen, and feed. Estimation values and seasonal guidelines are based on the population-estimation cheat sheet by Dr. Pia Aumeier, Germany.

frameType.title: Frame type
frameType.description: Choose the frame format used in your hive — it determines the estimation values per eighth.
frameType.customDescription: Enter your own estimation values for one eighth of a comb side.

counts.title: Counts — total eighths
counts.description: Sum the eighths of comb area across all comb sides for each category. One eighth holds about {{bees}} bees ({{frame}}). Brood and stores are optional — they are needed for split evaluation and feed assessment.

fields.beesHint: comb area covered with a layer of bees, both sides counted

result.title: Result
result.empty: Enter your eighth counts above to see the estimate.
result.emergeNote: Within the next 12 days, ~{{cells}} bees will emerge from the capped worker brood — roughly {{gassen}} additional Wabengassen. Expand the hive in time.

evaluation.title: Seasonal evaluation
evaluation.description: Compare the measured colony against the seasonal benchmarks by Dr. Pia Aumeier, Germany.
evaluation.selectPrompt: Select a context to evaluate the colony.
evaluation.splitNote: A broodless mating split (Begattungsableger) created by mid-May needs only ≥ 1,000 bees and no brood.

winterAug.note: Applies to established colonies in mid-August: a colony this strong hangs through into the floor across more than 5 Wabengassen. Weaker, shrinking colonies and this year's splits are assessed at the end of October instead.

winterOct.ready: Ready for winter — ~{{bees}} bees on {{gassen}} Wabengassen (5 or more occupied gaps; over 5,000 bees).
winterOct.minimal: Minimal wintering strength — ~{{bees}} bees on 4 Wabengassen (around 5,000 bees).
winterOct.atRisk: Strongly at risk of winter loss — ~{{bees}} bees on {{gassen}} Wabengassen (3 or fewer occupied gaps; under 5,000 bees). Combine or dissolve.
winterOct.note: Assess as late as possible, after a cold night (≈ 5 °C), by counting the Wabengassen occupied by bees.

reference.title: One eighth of a {{frame}} comb side contains…

pullFrames.title: Pull out each frame
pullFrames.description: Remove one frame at a time. Look straight at each side and estimate in eighths how much of the comb surface is covered with bees, brood, pollen, or feed.

bothSides.title: Count both sides
bothSides.description: Each frame has two sides. A frame fully covered with bees on both sides adds 16 eighths to the total.

roundDown.title: Round down when in doubt
roundDown.description: Underestimating is safer than overestimating — round to the nearest lower eighth.

capped.title: Capped worker brood
capped.description: All currently capped worker cells emerge within 0–12 days.

open.title: Open worker brood
open.description: Open worker cells emerge within 13–21 days.

drone.title: Drone brood
drone.description: Capped drone cells emerge within 0–14 days, open drone cells within 15–24 days.

ruleOfThumb.title: Rule of thumb
ruleOfThumb.description: About 1,000 emerged bees fill one Wabengasse — expand the hive before the colony outgrows it.

yearRound.title: Throughout the year
yearRound.description: At least 1 kg of feed at all times — for production colonies and splits alike.

warmRegion.title: Wintering, warm region (e.g. NRW)
warmRegion.description: Production colonies: 13 kg pure sugar = 18 kg / 13 L syrup ≈ 8 full Zander combs. Splits: 10 kg sugar = 14 kg / 10 L syrup ≈ 6 full Zander combs.

coolRegion.title: Wintering, cool region (e.g. BW, Bavaria)
coolRegion.description: Production colonies: 20 kg pure sugar = 28 kg / 20 L syrup ≈ 12 full Zander combs. Splits: 15 kg sugar = 21 kg / 15 L syrup ≈ 9 full Zander combs.

spring.title: Before the spring flow (Feb–Mar)
spring.description: In a wet, cold early spring: at least 10 kg of feed remaining.

queen.title: Queen — 16 days
queen.description: From the egg (swarm or silent supersedure): 3 d egg + 5 d larva + 8 d pupa. Emergency cells started from a ~2-day-old larva emerge after just 11 days. One queen per colony.

worker.title: Worker — 21 days
worker.description: From the fertilized egg: 3 d egg + 6 d larva + 12 d pupa. A colony holds 5,000–40,000 workers.

drone.title: Drone — 24 days
drone.description: From the unfertilized egg: 3 d egg + 7 d larva + 14 d pupa. A colony holds 0–1,000 drones.

broodTimeline.title: Brood Development Timeline
broodTimeline.description: Explore the development stages of queen, worker, and drone bees from egg to adult
broodTimeline.intro: Visualize honey bee brood development from egg to adult. Queens emerge in 16 days, workers in 21, and drones in 24 — split across four stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult. Pick a starting date to project capping and emergence days for inspection planning.
broodTimeline.beeTypeDescription: Select a caste to view its development timeline
broodTimeline.dateSelectorDescription: Optionally select when eggs were laid to see calendar dates on the timeline
broodTimeline.workerEggDesc: Tiny white egg upright in cell
broodTimeline.workerLarvaDesc: Fed royal jelly, then bee bread & honey
broodTimeline.fact1Description: The only difference between a queen and a worker is diet. Larvae fed exclusively royal jelly develop into queens — completing development in just 16 days compared to 21 for workers.
broodTimeline.fact2Description: Drones take the longest to develop (24 days) and come from unfertilised eggs. They have no father but do have a grandfather!
broodTimeline.fact3Description: All three castes share the same 3-day egg stage. The queen lays each egg vertically in the cell; it gradually tilts over before hatching.
broodTimeline.fact4Description: Workers cap queen cells on day 8, worker cells on day 9, and drone cells on day 10. Spotting capped cells helps identify what's developing inside.

items.question: How long does it take for a honey bee to develop?
items.answer: Queens emerge after 16 days, workers after 21 days, and drones after 24 days. All three pass through four stages — egg (3 days), larva, pupa, and adult — but the larval and pupal stages differ in length by caste.

items.question: When are worker bee cells capped?
items.answer: Worker brood cells are capped on day 9, when the larva spins its cocoon and enters the pupal stage. Worker bees emerge on day 21.

items.question: When are queen cells capped and when do queens emerge?
items.answer: Queen cells are capped on day 8 and virgin queens emerge on day 16 from the egg being laid. Queens develop fastest because they are fed royal jelly throughout the larval stage.

items.question: How long do drones take to emerge?
items.answer: Drones take 24 days from egg to emergence — the longest of any caste. Drone cells are capped around day 10.

swarmManagement.title: Swarm Management
swarmManagement.intro: Free reference guides for honey bee swarm control. Compare proven methods like the Demaree, plan follow-up inspections, and reduce the risk of losing colonies to swarming during the busy spring season.

demaree.title: Demaree Method
demaree.description: A swarm-control method that separates the queen from most brood, reducing congestion while keeping the colony strong and productive.
demaree.detail: Review the method, generate a follow-up plan, and save the checkpoints as scheduled inspections.

pagden.title: Pagden split
pagden.description: Placeholder for a future guide covering the classic artificial swarm split.

artificialSwarm.title: Artificial swarm
artificialSwarm.description: Placeholder for a future guide focused on moving flying bees and brood apart.

aside.title: Choosing a method
aside.description: Use this section to compare swarm-control approaches and pick the one that fits your hive setup, labour rhythm, and management style.
points: Start with a method whose follow-up schedule you can reliably meet during the swarm season.
Prefer techniques that match your preferred brood arrangement, available equipment, and comfort finding queens quickly.
Once you choose a method, open its guide page for the step-by-step reference and planning tools.
aside.criticalTimingLead: The first follow-up is the point where timing matters most.
aside.criticalTimingDetail: If that queen-cell check slips beyond the recommended window, cells may be close to emergence and the colony can quickly return to swarm mode.

demaree.title: Demaree Method
demaree.description: A reference Demaree workflow for strong colonies showing swarm pressure, with practical follow-up timing built into an inspection planner.
demaree.intro: The Demaree method is a classic swarm-control technique for strong honey bee colonies that keeps the colony intact while relieving congestion. The queen stays below an excluder with fresh comb to lay; the bulk of the brood is moved above two honey supers, separating the queen pheromone trail from the broodnest. Follow up around day 7–8, day 14–15, and day 21–22 to remove emergency queen cells before they emerge.
demaree.overviewLead: Use the Demaree method when the colony is strong, the queen is laying well, and you want to ease swarm pressure without breaking the production colony into separate units.
overviewPoints: Read through the full method before opening the hive, then carry it out consistently so the brood moves, box order, and flying-bee return pattern all support the same plan.
Keep the laying queen below the excluder with room to continue laying, move most brood above, and give the colony space upward so congestion does not rebuild immediately.

Once the initial setup is finished, generate the follow-up plan from the actual setup date and treat the warning banners as prompts to review your timing, especially around the day 7 to 8 queen-cell check.
prerequisites: Check that the hive is a strong colony with enough bees and brood to justify the method.
Confirm there is clear swarm pressure, such as congestion, charged queen cells, or a colony building hard into the flow.
Make sure you can find and confidently retain the laying queen in the lower brood box.
Have a queen excluder ready, with at least two honey supers available to stack above it.
Prepare a spare brood box with drawn comb so the colony can be rearranged without leaving the queen cramped.
Choose a settled weather window so the colony can reorganise without a prolonged interruption.
demaree.stepsDescription: If unexpected queen cells are found during an inspection follow the steps below

items.text: Find a spare brood box with foundation or drawn comb.
children: Using foundation may reduce honey crop as the bees will use a lot of stores for wax generation.

items.text: Move the brood box(es) off the hive floor and place the new empty box at the bottom.
Inspect the old brood box(es) and locate the Queen.
Take her and the frame she was found on and place it in the centre of the new box, removing any queen cells that are on that frame.
If using double brood, manipulate the frames so that all brood is in a single box.
children: If there are more than will fit, add the remainder to the bottom box with the queen and redistribute the empty frames.

items.text: Make sure to knock down all queen cells.
children: With the queen safe you can shake bees off the frames to make sure you find them all.

items.text: (Double Brood) - You should now have three brood boxes.
children: Box with the queen, mostly empty directly on the hive floor.
Box with no brood - place this on top of the queen to retain double brood status.
Box with all the brood - keep this aside for now.

items.text: Put the queen excluder on top of the new brood boxes.
Add the supers (minimum 2, add extra if more space is required).
Place an additional queen excluder over the supers.
The box with all the brood now goes on top of the supers.
Fit crown board and roof and the job is done.

demaree.advantagesDescription: The Demaree method relieves swarm pressure by redistributing brood and flying bees without splitting the colony into separate working units.
advantages: The queen keeps laying in the lower box while the upper brood area loses the support pattern that usually feeds swarm preparation.
Flying bees continue to return to the original stand, which helps maintain activity below and reduces congestion in the brood moved upward.
Because the colony stays together, the method can preserve honey-gathering momentum better than a full artificial split.

followUp.task: Inspect the brood-above-super box for queen cells and remove any that have been started.
Review again for any late-started queen cells and confirm the brood arrangement still supports the Demaree setup.
Carry out a final review to decide whether the colony can be normalised or still needs close swarm-pressure monitoring.

pros: Works well as a swarm control method.
Can be used for queen-right cell raising when queen rearing, without the need to set up another colony.
Good brood combs can be drawn out in the top box.
The effect is similar to an artificial swarm, but the colony is kept together.
cons: As with other swarm control methods one queen cell missed in the top box may result in a swarm.
Large colonies result, so probably not a good method for beginners as bees need to be shaken off all combs.

items.question: What is the Demaree method?
items.answer: The Demaree method is a classic swarm-control technique that vertically separates the laying queen from most of the brood within a single hive. The queen stays below a queen excluder on largely empty comb; the brood is moved above two or more honey supers so the colony stays together while swarm pressure eases.

items.question: When should I use the Demaree method?
items.answer: Use the Demaree method on strong colonies showing clear swarm pressure — heavy congestion, charged queen cells, or a colony pushing hard into a flow — when you want to keep the colony intact rather than splitting it.

items.question: When should I inspect after a Demaree setup?
items.answer: Inspect at day 7-8, day 14-15, and day 21-22 after the setup. The day 7-8 check is the most critical — emergency queen cells started during the swarm impulse must be removed before they approach emergence around day 14-16.

items.question: Does the Demaree method create a separate split or nucleus colony?
items.answer: No. Unlike an artificial swarm or Pagden split, the Demaree method keeps all the bees in one hive. The colony remains a single working unit which often preserves honey-gathering momentum better than splitting.

items.question: What equipment do I need for the Demaree method?
items.answer: You need a queen excluder (sometimes two), a spare brood box with drawn comb or foundation, and at least two honey supers to stack between the queen and the relocated brood.

items.question: What is the biggest risk with the Demaree method?
items.answer: Missing a queen cell in the upper brood box during the day 7-8 check is the biggest risk — the colony can still swarm if a virgin queen emerges above the excluder. Shaking bees off every brood frame helps spot all cells.

planner.title: Demaree inspection planner
planner.description: Generate the standard checkpoints, adjust dates if needed, and save them as scheduled inspections for one hive.
planner.emptyState: Choose a hive and setup date, then generate the follow-up checkpoints.
planner.saveSuccess_one: Saved {{count}} Demaree checkpoint as scheduled inspection.
planner.saveSuccess_other: Saved {{count}} Demaree checkpoints as scheduled inspections.
planner.partialSave: Saved {{successCount}} checkpoints, but {{failedCount}} failed. Review upcoming inspections before retrying.
planner.saveError: Unable to save the Demaree plan. Some inspections may not have been created.
planner.noHives: You need at least one hive in the active apiary before you can create a Demaree plan.
planner.signInTitle: Sign in to plan your inspections
planner.signInDescription: Create a free account to generate Demaree checkpoints, link them to one of your hives, and save them as scheduled inspections.
planner.atAGlanceDescription: Quick reference for the standard Demaree follow-ups.

setup.title: Initial Demaree setup
setup.summary: Perform the brood rearrangement, confirm the laying queen is below the excluder, and leave the colony with room above.
checklist: Confirm the laying queen is in the lower brood box.
Place the brood and supers in the planned Demaree order.
Record any details you want visible at the next inspection.

queenCellCheck.title: Queen-cell check
queenCellCheck.summary: Inspect the upper brood area around day 7 to 8 and remove emergency queen cells before they approach emergence.
checklist: Inspect every brood frame above the excluder for queen cells.
Remove queen cells started during the swarm impulse.
Confirm the queen still has room and activity below.

secondQueenCellCheck.title: Second queen-cell check
secondQueenCellCheck.summary: Recheck about a week later to catch any late-started cells and make sure the brood nest remains balanced.
checklist: Look again for newly started or missed queen cells.
Check whether brood and stores need to be rebalanced.
Note whether the colony is settling or still crowding upward.

colonyReview.title: Colony review
colonyReview.summary: Confirm swarm pressure has eased, decide whether to normalise the stack, and record any next steps.
checklist: Assess whether the colony can return to a more normal arrangement.
Check space, brood pattern, and nectar intake across the stack.
Schedule any additional inspection you want beyond the standard plan.

lateQueenCellCheck.title: First queen-cell check is later than day 8
lateQueenCellCheck.description: This delay increases the risk of queen cells reaching emergence before you remove them. Try to keep the first follow-up within the 7 to 8 day window.

unsafeCheckpointSpacing.title: Checkpoint spacing looks risky
unsafeCheckpointSpacing.description: These follow-up inspections are spread unusually far apart or packed too tightly, which can make queen-cell control and brood review less reliable.

illogicalScheduleOrder.title: Checkpoint order is no longer chronological
illogicalScheduleOrder.description: One of the later checkpoints is scheduled on or before an earlier visit. Reorder the dates before saving the plan.

varroaManagement.title: Varroa Management Plan
varroaManagement.intro: A season-long varroa control schedule for production colonies (Wirtschaftsvölker) and nucleus colonies (Ableger), based on the proven concept by Dr. Pia Aumeier, Dr. Gerhard Liebig, and Dr. Otto Boecking. The key rule: never treat on a fixed schedule — measure the natural mite fall first and treat only when a threshold is exceeded.

legend.title: Legend:
legend.threshold: red box = treat only above the stated natural mite fall per day

production.title: Cut drone brood
items: At cherry blossom, expand every colony with a queen excluder and honey super, and hang an empty drone frame in the upper brood box between the outermost comb and drawn combs.
Cut the frame out after 14 days at the earliest and 24 days at the latest — never let the drones emerge. Repeat as often as possible from April to July.

nucleus.title: Spray with lactic acid
items: In the broodless phase right after the nucleus is made up, spray the bees on every comb (both sides, about 3 bursts per side) with 15% lactic acid.
With heavy varroa infestation, repeat the treatment after a few days.
nucleus.note: Wear gloves, long clothing, and safety glasses. Sprayed colonies must not be used for honey production in the same year.

production.monitor: After the last honey harvest: insert the sticky board for exactly 3 days and count the natural mite fall.

threshold.condition: Natural mite fall above 10 varroa per day:

production.note: Very rarely necessary. Below the threshold, do not treat — spare the brood and bees.

threshold.condition: Natural mite fall above 5 varroa per day:

nucleus.note: Very rarely necessary. Below the threshold, do not treat.

production.title: Start late-summer care
items: Condense the colony by removing the lower brood box (comb hygiene).
1× treatment with formic acid.
Then feed the winter stores in one large portion.
production.note: Alternative: make the colony broodless with “divide and treat” and use oxalic acid instead of formic acid — see the detailed guide below.

nucleus.title: No treatment
items: Simply keep feeding small portions and let the colony develop and draw out its combs.

production.monitor: After feeding is finished: 3-day mite-fall count.

threshold.condition: Natural mite fall above 5 varroa per day:

production.note: Below the threshold, no further treatment is needed.

threshold.condition: Natural mite fall above 1 varroa per day:

nucleus.note: Winter feeding follows after the treatment.

production.monitor: In the broodless winter period: 3-day mite-fall count.

threshold.condition: Natural mite fall above 1 varroa per day:

production.note: Trickle 30–50 ml of 3.5% oxalic acid solution onto the bees in the occupied seams. Below the threshold, no treatment is needed.

nucleus.monitor: In the broodless winter period: 3-day mite-fall count.

threshold.condition: Natural mite fall above 1 varroa per day:

nucleus.note: Trickle 30–50 ml of 3.5% oxalic acid solution. Below the threshold, no treatment is needed.

divideAndTreat.title: Divide and treat (Teilen und Behandeln)
divideAndTreat.summary: Had enough of formic acid? Divide and treat makes a production colony broodless after the last honey harvest, so a single, weather-independent oxalic or lactic acid treatment hits every mite. Here is the flightling and broodling method, step by step.

intro.0: In late July a strong production colony holds up to 30,000 bees and 30,000 brood cells. The more brood, the harder it is to get a good treatment result with formic acid without brood damage — especially in cool, damp weather. Divide and treat, refined from the Celle rotation system in the German “Betriebsweisen im Vergleich” project, sidesteps the problem entirely: the colony is split into two broodless halves that can each be treated reliably with oxalic or lactic acid, independent of the weather.
intro.1: You split the production colony into a queenright flightling and a queenless broodling. All you need is a spare floor, a crown board or foil, a lid, and a queen cage with a candy or marshmallow closure. Five apiary visits and about 15 minutes of total work de-mite the colony in two moves, allow complete comb renewal, and — if you want — raise a young queen and a new colony on the side.

flightling.title: Flightling (Flugling)
flightling.subtitle: Queenright flight-bee colony
points: The harvested honey super with its bees, placed on the original floor at the original location — all flight bees return to it.
Keeps the old queen, caged under a candy closure until the workers free her.
Broodless immediately: every mite sits on the bees, so one treatment on day X+2 catches them all.
Two days after splitting it holds up to two thirds of the bees; the queen is already released and laying again.

broodling.title: Broodling (Brutling)
broodling.subtitle: Queenless brood colony
points: Both brood boxes on a new floor — either directly on top of the flightling (same flight direction) or a few metres away.
Contains all the brood and roughly 90% of the mites, but loses its flight bees to the flightling.
Raises an emergency queen from the youngest larvae; in about 90% of cases she is successfully mated.
Broodless on day X+21, once all the worker brood has emerged — the moment to treat.

preparation.title: Preparation
items: Between mid July and mid August, harvest the summer honey. Take the queen excluder home and slide the sticky board in for the mite-fall count.
If possible, put the extracted honey super back on one day later and let the bees clean it for two to three days.

Count the natural mite fall from the 2–3 days the board was in: it should be no more than 30 mites per day. More heavily infested colonies can collapse before day X+21 — treat those with formic acid instead.

dayZero.title: Split the colony
items: In good weather, take the colony apart. Set the honey super with the bees inside it onto the colony's original floor — this becomes the flightling. If it holds no feed at all, hang in a feed comb (1–2 kg, usually found at the edge of the upper brood box).
Find the queen in the brood chamber (she is usually in the upper box), cage her under a candy or marshmallow closure, and hang the cage into the flightling.

Can't find the queen, or are the bees too defensive? Hang one comb of open brood into the flightling instead and swap it for the queen two days later — she is far easier to find in the bee-depleted broodling. Day X+21 then becomes X+23.

Place both brood boxes (older combs at the bottom) on a new floor, either directly on top of the flightling facing the same way, or several metres away — this becomes the broodling. Never right next to the flightling, or the flight bees drift back into the bigger unit.
Reduce the entrances of both units to about 2 cm — the broodling has neither queen nor flight bees for a few days and is highly prone to robbing.
Never give liquid feed on splitting day: it triggers robbing.

dayTwo.title: Treat the flightling
items: Up to two thirds of the bees now sit in the flightling; the queen is free and laying.

Early in the morning, while the bees sit tightly, trickle 100–200 ml of 3.5% oxalic acid solution — or better, pull every comb during the day and spray the bees with 3% oxalic acid solution (spraying works better than trickling in summer).
One treatment is enough: about 90% of the mites sit in the brood and on the nurse bees, i.e. in the broodling.
If no late nectar flow is expected, feed the flightling up to 6 litres of syrup now — then nothing more until October, so brood rearing isn't choked.

dayTwentyOne.title: Treat the broodling
items: All worker brood has now emerged, so the broodling is broodless. The emergency queen has usually hatched but is not laying yet — don't search for eggs unnecessarily.

Take the broodling apart and condense it onto a single box: remove the old combs (classically the 10 older, lower ones) and shake their bees into the broodling or in front of its entrance. If the brood combs hold forest or heather honey, you can harvest all of them and put the colony onto foundation plus one feed comb.
Treat exactly like the flightling before: trickle or spray with oxalic acid in the broodless state.
A second treatment four days later is often needed here: if more than 5,000 mites fall within a week of the treatment, re-treat immediately.
Feed after the treatment — or wait until the young queen is confirmed laying; a laying queen lowers the robbing risk.

dayTwentyEight.title: Check the young queen
items: Was the past week mating-flight weather (20 °C, sunny, calm)? Then 9 out of 10 broodlings now have a young, laying queen — check for eggs.
If there is no laying queen, dissolve the broodling by shaking its bees off the combs in front of the flightling's entrance.

october.title: Reunite — or overwinter separately
items: If you want one strong colony: simply set the broodling on top of the flightling, no newspaper needed. As a rule the queen of the upper colony survives — to be sure a specific queen survives, remove the other one beforehand. Late reunification gives rock-solid winter colonies headed by a young queen.
If you keep both units, feed each so it goes into winter with at least 15 kg of stores (roughly 15 litres of syrup if the combs are empty), ideally split into an early and a late portion.

advantages: Weather-independent varroa control on a freely chosen schedule — no formic acid evaporation worries.
Complete comb hygiene and comb renewal in the same operation.
Honey can be harvested even from honey-clogged brood chambers (including late forest or heather honey).
No unwanted emergency queen cells to hunt down and break, unlike caging the queen for weeks.
No risk to the queen from weeks of caging: about one in five caged queens is rejected by her workers after release — this method avoids that entirely.
Usually exceptionally strong winter colonies with young queens when both halves are reunited in October.
Alternatively, a free colony increase in July/August instead of reuniting.

caution.title: When not to use it
caution.body: Count the natural mite fall before splitting. Above roughly 30 mites per day the brood is so heavily infested that the emerging broodling bees die quickly and the broodling collapses before day X+21 — treat such colonies with formic acid right away instead.
caution.robbing: And never feed liquid feed on the day of splitting — the queenless, guard-poor broodling would be robbed out immediately.

monitoring.title: Measuring natural mite fall (Gemülldiagnose)
monitoring.summary: Every treatment decision in this plan hangs on one number: the natural mite fall per day. Here is how to measure it correctly.
monitoring.intro: Too early, too often, too late — late-summer varroa treatment is rarely well matched to the colony's needs. Whoever records the natural mite fall with a suitable sticky board knows exactly where each colony stands, fights the mite precisely, and avoids unpleasant surprises.
items: Use a screened floor (3 mm mesh) under which a sticky board can be slid in bee-tight.
Remove any entrance reducers or mouse guards before diagnosing.
Slide the board in for exactly 3 days before every planned treatment (first time: end of July, before the first treatment).
Pull the board and count the dark and light-brown mites — use a magnifier if needed.
Divide the count by 3: that is the natural mite fall per day, the number every threshold in the plan refers to.
monitoring.conversionTitle: What one dead mite per day means
conversion: End of July – mid August: roughly 150–250 living mites in the colony
Early – mid September: roughly 250–350 living mites
End of November: roughly 500 living mites
monitoring.note: Don't leave the board in permanently — it attracts ants and wax moths and only needs to be in for diagnosis (or during a formic acid treatment). Washing bee samples is more laborious and no more reliable than the debris diagnosis.

aside.thresholdsDescription: Treatment is urgently required at a natural mite fall above ... per day:
aside.thresholdsNote: Natural mite fall per day, measured over exactly 3 days with a sticky board under a screened floor.
aside.principleBody: The recipe's success factor: record the natural mite fall with screened floors and sticky boards instead of treating on a fixed calendar. Every treatment is matched to the actual infestation and the colony's development. Used nationwide since 2008 in 140 hobby, professional, and institute apiaries, this approach kept colony losses at 7% or less — with no chemicals during the nectar flow and no residue-forming or resistance-building varroacides.
aside.sourceBody: Concept by Dr. Pia Aumeier, developed with Dr. Gerhard Liebig and Dr. Otto Boecking at Ruhr-Universität Bochum in the “Imkerberatung NRW” project; divide-and-treat method as published in Deutsches Bienen-Journal (DNB 07/2017).

reports.selectApiary: Select an apiary to view reports

widget.noApiary: Select an apiary to view statistics

footer.tagline: Free, open-source beekeeping management. Making the craft simpler, more productive, and a little more enjoyable.
footer.builtWith: Built with care for beekeepers everywhere.

ai.title:
Inspect with your voice,
not your keyboard.
ai.lede: Hive Pal records audio during inspections, transcribes it automatically, and uses AI to draft structured notes — so the busywork happens while you’re still at the apiary.

record.title: Record at the hive
record.body: Tap record on your phone, talk through what you see on each frame, and move on. Recordings upload straight into the inspection — even if you lose signal mid-yard.

transcribe.title: Automatic transcription
transcribe.body: Whisper-powered speech-to-text turns every recording into a searchable transcript attached to the inspection. Multilingual out of the box.

draft.title: AI-drafted notes
draft.body: Local LLMs extract queen sightings, brood patterns, treatments, and observations into a pre-filled inspection — you just review and save.

ai.assuranceBody: The AI stack runs on open-source models (faster-whisper + Ollama) and can be self-hosted alongside your data — or run as a pull worker on your home machine.

meta.title: Hive Pal — AI-Powered Beekeeping Management Software
meta.description: Manage your beehives efficiently with Hive Pal. Record voice notes at the hive and let AI transcribe and draft your inspections. Open source, self-hostable, and free for early adopters.
meta.ogTitle: Hive Pal — AI-Powered Beekeeping Management Software
meta.ogDescription: Free, open-source beekeeping platform with AI voice inspections. Record audio in the apiary and let AI draft your inspection notes automatically.

hero.title:
Your open-source
beekeeping companion.
hero.lede: Record voice notes at the hive. Let AI transcribe and draft your inspections automatically — so you keep your hands on the frames, not the keyboard.

features.label: Modern beekeeping
features.title: Everything you need to <accent>tend</accent> your hives.
features.lede: Professional tools designed by beekeepers, for beekeepers — to keep colonies healthy, productive, and well-documented through every season.
features.pillars: Four pillars · built into one platform

apiary.title: Apiary management
apiary.body: Organize and track multiple apiaries with detailed location information, weather data, and site-specific notes.

inspections.title: Hive inspections
inspections.body: Schedule and record detailed inspections with customizable checklists, photos, and health assessments.

queen.title: Queen tracking
queen.body: Monitor queen performance, breeding records, and replacement schedules to maintain strong colonies.

harvest.title: Harvest management
harvest.body: Track honey production, processing dates, and yields across seasons and hive locations.

run.label: How to run it
run.title: Self-host, or let us <accent>run it</accent> for you.
run.lede: Two ways to use Hive Pal — both free, both run the same open-source code.

selfHosted.title: Self-hosted
selfHosted.body: Full control over your data and infrastructure. Run it on your own server, your laptop, a Raspberry Pi — wherever suits you.
items: MIT licensed — read, fork, ship
Host anywhere Docker runs
Your data stays on your hardware
Community support via GitHub

hosted.title: Hosted by us
hosted.body: Zero setup required. We run the servers, handle updates, and keep things ticking. Same open-source app — just on our hardware.
items: Sign up and go — no installation
Automatic updates & backups
Best-effort uptime, monitored
Community support via GitHub

tools.label: Free tools
tools.title: Free tools, no signup.
tools.lede: Handy calculators and planners you can use directly in the browser — bookmark them for the apiary.

syrup.title: Sugar Syrup Calculator
syrup.body: Precise sugar and water amounts for 1:1, 3:2, or 2:1 syrup at any container size. Metric or imperial.

brood.title: Brood Development Timeline
brood.body: Visualize queen, worker, and drone brood stages with day counts and project dates from any starting day.

swarm.title: Swarm Management
swarm.body: Step-by-step Demaree swarm-control workflow with follow-up timing and an inspection planner.

liebefelder.title: Liebefelder Estimation Method
liebefelder.body: Estimate colony strength — bees, brood, pollen and feed — by counting comb eighths, with seasonal evaluation guidelines.

varroa.title: Varroa Management Plan
varroa.body: Season-long treatment schedule for production colonies and nuclei with mite-fall thresholds, plus the divide-and-treat method explained step by step.

cta.title: Give it a <accent>try</accent>.
cta.lede: Sign up for the hosted version, or grab the source and run it yourself. Either way, it’s free.

meta.title: Features — Everything Hive Pal Does for Beekeepers
meta.description: Explore every Hive Pal feature: AI voice inspections with automatic transcription, hive and queen management, batch inspections, planning and reports, harvest tracking, collaboration, and open-source, self-hostable, privacy-first software.
meta.ogTitle: Hive Pal Features — AI Voice Inspections & Beekeeping Management
meta.ogDescription: AI voice inspections, hive & queen management, planning, reports, collaboration, and open-source self-hosting. See everything Hive Pal does.

hero.title: Everything you need to <accent>run the bees</accent>.
hero.lede: Hive Pal brings voice-first AI inspections, colony and queen records, planning, reports, and collaboration into a single open-source platform — free to use and yours to host.

inspections.label: Inspections & record-keeping
inspections.title: Every inspection, captured in detail
inspections.lede: Record what you see at the hive with structured, repeatable inspections — then find it again the moment you need it.

structured.title: Structured inspections
structured.body: Log brood pattern, stores, temperament, space, and queen status with consistent fields, so colonies are comparable across visits and seasons.

quickChecks.title: Quick checks
quickChecks.body: A lightweight inspection flow for fast yard walk-throughs when you only need to note the essentials and move on.

batch.title: Batch inspections
batch.body: Inspect a whole apiary in one sitting — step through every hive in sequence without re-opening each record by hand.

scheduled.title: Scheduled inspections & reminders
scheduled.body: Plan future visits and get release-note and task reminders so nothing slips between sessions.

photos.title: Photos & observations
photos.body: Attach photos and free-text notes to any inspection to document disease signs, comb, or anything worth a second look.

actions.title: Actions & treatments
actions.body: Record feeding, treatments, and management actions as part of the inspection, building a full history per colony.

colony.label: Colony & hive management
colony.title: Organize apiaries, hives, and queens
colony.lede: A clear model of your operation — from yards down to individual frames and queen lineages.

apiaries.title: Multiple apiaries
apiaries.body: Group hives by location with site-specific notes and local weather context for each yard.

hiveConfig.title: Hive configuration
hiveConfig.body: Track boxes, frames, and configuration per hive so the record reflects the real equipment in the field.

queens.title: Queen tracking
queens.body: Follow each queen’s marking, origin, lineage, performance, and replacement history to keep colonies strong.

equipment.title: Equipment planning
equipment.body: Plan and account for boxes, frames, and gear across your hives so you know what to build before the season.

qrCodes.title: QR codes
qrCodes.body: Generate and print QR labels for hives, then scan in the apiary to jump straight to the right record.

hiveScale.title: Hive scale monitoring
hiveScale.body: Connect hive scales to follow weight trends — nectar flow, consumption, and overwintering at a glance.

planning.label: Planning & insight
planning.title: See the season, not just the day
planning.lede: Turn your records into a plan and a picture of how the operation is doing.

calendar.title: Calendar
calendar.body: A unified calendar of inspections and scheduled tasks across every apiary, so the week ahead is always visible.

reports.title: Reports & analytics
reports.body: Roll your data up into colony-health and productivity reports to spot trends and weak hives early.

harvest.title: Harvest tracking
harvest.body: Record honey harvests, processing dates, and yields across hives and seasons to measure real output.

assistant.title: AI assistant
assistant.body: Ask questions about your apiary and get help making sense of your records, right inside the app.

bulkActions.title: Bulk actions
bulkActions.body: Apply the same action across many hives at once instead of editing them one by one.

collaboration.label: Collaboration & sharing
collaboration.title: Beekeeping is rarely a solo job
collaboration.lede: Work the bees with partners, clubs, or mentees — and share what matters without handing over the keys.

sharing.title: Apiary sharing
sharing.body: Invite other beekeepers to collaborate on an apiary so the whole team works from the same records.

inviteLinks.title: Invite links
inviteLinks.body: Share a join link to bring someone into an apiary quickly, without manual account juggling.

sharedViews.title: Shared views
sharedViews.body: Give read access to colonies or data so a mentor or club can follow along without editing.

data.label: Your data, your rules
data.title: Open source, private, and portable
data.lede: Hive Pal is free software you can run yourself — your records stay yours.

openSource.title: Open source (MIT)
openSource.body: The entire app is MIT-licensed. Read it, fork it, audit it, or contribute — no black boxes.

selfHostable.title: Self-hostable
selfHostable.body: Run it anywhere Docker runs: a server, a laptop, or a Raspberry Pi. The hosted version runs the same code.

importExport.title: Import & export
importExport.body: Move your data in and out freely. Your beekeeping history is never locked inside the app.

privacyAI.title: Privacy-first AI
privacyAI.body: The voice and AI stack uses open models (faster-whisper + Ollama) and can run on your own hardware — no third-party cloud required.

multilingual.title: Multilingual
multilingual.body: Use Hive Pal in your language, with community translations managed through Weblate and growing over time.

pwa.title: Installable & offline-ready
pwa.body: Install it as a PWA on your phone and keep working in the apiary even when the signal drops.

tools.label: No signup required
tools.title: Free tools you can use right now
tools.lede: Calculators and planners that work in the browser, no account needed — a taste of how Hive Pal thinks about beekeeping.

cta.title: Ready to <accent>try it</accent>?
cta.lede: Sign up for the hosted version, or grab the source and run it yourself. Either way, it’s free.

meta.title: Free Beekeeping Tools — Hive Pal
meta.description: Free, open-source calculators and reference guides for beekeepers. Sugar syrup calculator, brood development timeline, and swarm management workflows.
meta.ogDescription: Free calculators and reference guides for beekeepers — no signup required.
